summary: Julia Evans shares slides from a 10-minute talk at Recurse Center about common blogging myths that discourage people from writing, including beliefs that you need to be original, expert, or always correct. She also describes a debugging technique of adding print statements directly to library code to understand confusing errors. The post is brief and practical, combining encouragement for aspiring bloggers with a concrete debugging tip.
key_points: Common blogging myths (needing originality, expertise, perfection) unnecessarily discourage people from writing
Adding print statements directly to library source code is an underused but effective debugging technique
90% of the time the bug is in your own code, and reading library internals helps you understand how the library actually works
Languages with accessible dependency directories (node_modules, site-packages) make this technique especially easy
money_quotes: Sometimes when I'm debugging, I'm using a library, and I'm getting an error, and I have NO IDEA why and all the Googling in the world just does not tell me anything useful.
90% of the time the problem is with my code and it just helps me understand how the library is actually supposed to work.

This works really well with Ruby / Node / Python / Go – anywhere where it's normal to have a directory like node_modules with all the code for all your dependencies where you can easily edit & rerun the code.
key_insight: When stuck on a library error, adding print statements directly to the library's source code is a simple but powerful way to understand what's actually happening.
